top_stories.title: Oil prices rise after ships attacked near Strait of Hormuz
top_stories.description: Experts have warned that a prolonged conflict could push global energy prices even higher.
top_stories.scraped_content: Oil prices rise after ships attacked near Strait of Hormuz 53 minutes ago Share Save Daniel Thomas , Ben Hatton and Peter Hoskins Share Save Getty Images File photo of shipping in the Strait of Hormuz, which has now ground to a halt Global oil prices have risen after at least three ships were attacked near the Strait of Hormuz, as Iran continues to launch strikes across the Middle East in response to ongoing attacks by the US and Israel. Two vessels have been struck, and an "unknown projectile" was reported to have "exploded in very close proximity" to a third, the UK Maritime Trade Operations Centre (UKMTO) said. Iran has warned ships not to pass through the strait, which carries about 20% of the world's oil and gas. International shipping has almost come to a standstill at the strait's entrance, with analysts warning that a prolonged conflict could push energy prices even higher. In early trade in Asia on Monday, global oil prices jumped by more than 10% before those gains eased during the morning. At a little before 07:00 GMT, Brent crude was more than 8% higher at $78. 82) a barrel, while US-traded oil was up by around 7. "The market isn't panicking", Saul Kavonic, head of energy research at MST Marquee told the BBC.
top_stories.enhanced_summary: Oil prices surged after multiple ships were attacked near the Strait of Hormuz, a critical passage for global energy supplies. Iran's actions, amidst escalating tensions with the US and Israel, have prompted warnings and significantly disrupted international shipping.
top_stories.why_matters: This incident highlights the fragility of global energy markets and the potential for geopolitical instability in the Middle East to rapidly impact commodity prices. Businesses should anticipate increased transportation costs and potential supply chain disruptions, underscoring the need for diversified sourcing and risk management strategies.

top_stories.title: Allies of US in the Gulf bear brunt of Iran attacks
top_stories.description: Iran's attacks on Gulf Arab states suggest the Islamic Republic is targeting not just the US military but also civilian infrastructure.
top_stories.scraped_content: Allies of US in the Gulf bear brunt of Iran attacks 9 hours ago Share Save Frank Gardner BBC security correspondent Share Save Reuters Cities in the Gulf states, like Abu Dhabi pictured here, were not built to withstand attacks from drones and ballistic missiles In the clear blue skies above Abu Dhabi, white contrails streak high above the sand-coloured villas and well-watered gardens. These are no incoming Dreamliners or Airbuses bringing in the next manifest of tourists and guest workers. They are incoming ballistic missiles, launched by the Emirates' giant neighbour just across the Gulf: Iran. As of Sunday afternoon, the United Arab Emirates (UAE) defence ministry said it had so far "dealt with" 165 incoming ballistic missiles, two cruise missiles and 541 Iranian drones. In Bahrain, a friend alerted me on Sunday morning that the airport was under attack after having a sleepless night. "Woken by huge bangs and wailing siren," he texted. "I think maybe around 20 booms and bangs. These are not familiar scenes in this region, but since this conflict began on Saturday morning, Iran appears to have expanded its target set from just hitting military targets, like the US Navy's 5th Fleet headquarters in Bahrain, to airports and other civilian sites.
top_stories.enhanced_summary: Iran has expanded its attacks in the Gulf region since a recent conflict began, targeting not just military sites but also civilian infrastructure in countries like the UAE and Bahrain. These US allies are now facing a barrage of ballistic missiles and drones, placing a significant strain on their defenses.
top_stories.why_matters: This escalation signals a worrying shift in regional stability, with Iran seemingly more willing to directly target US allies in the Gulf. Businesses operating in the UAE and Bahrain, particularly in aviation and tourism, could face increased security risks and disruptions, impacting investor confidence and long-term economic prospects in the region.

quick_reads.title: Iran's regime is still intact - the coming days will show if it can hold out
quick_reads.description: Iran's surviving leaders are in crisis mode, battling to project security as US and Israel strikes continue.
quick_reads.llm_reason: The stability of the Iranian regime is important; future instability may cause significant disruptions to global trade and investment.

quick_reads.title: Nine dead in missile attack on Israel as Iran strikes region
quick_reads.description: Several deaths are reported across the Middle East as Tehran retaliates for massive strikes by the US and Israel.
quick_reads.llm_reason: Regional instability, even if it doesn't directly impact NZ, has the potential to escalate and disrupt global markets.

top_stories.title: OpenAI reveals more details about its agreement with the Pentagon
top_stories.description: By CEO Sam Altman’s own admission, OpenAI’s deal with the Department of Defense was “definitely rushed,” and “the optics don’t look good.”
top_stories.scraped_content: Founder Summit 2026 in Boston:Don’t miss ticket savings of up to $300. Save up to $680 on your Disrupt 2026 pass. Latest AI Amazon Apps Biotech & Health Climate Cloud Computing Commerce Crypto Enterprise EVs Fintech Fundraising Gadgets Gaming Google Government & Policy Hardware Instagram Layoffs Media & Entertainment Meta Microsoft Privacy Robotics Security Social Space Startups TikTok Transportation Venture Staff Events Startup Battlefield StrictlyVC Newsletters Podcasts Videos Partner Content TechCrunch Brand Studio Crunchboard Contact Us By CEO Sam Altman’s own admission, OpenAI’s deal with the Department of Defense was “definitely rushed,” and “the optics don’t look good. ” Afternegotiations between Anthropic and the Pentagon fell throughon Friday, President Donald Trump directed federal agencies to stop using Anthropic’s technology aftera six-month transition period, and Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth said he was designating the AI company as a supply-chain risk. Then,OpenAI quickly announcedthat it had reached a deal of its own for models to be deployed in classified environments. With Anthropic saying it was drawing red lines around the use of its technology in fully autonomous weapons or mass domestic surveillance, and Altman saying OpenAI had the same red lines, there were some obvious questions: Was OpenAI being honest about its safeguards? Why was it able to reach a deal while Anthropic was not? So as OpenAI executives defended the agreement on social media, the company also publisheda blog post outlining its approach. In fact, the post pointed to three areas where it said OpenAI’s models cannot be used — mass domestic surveillance, autonomous weapon systems, and “high-stakes automated decisions (e. systems such as ‘social credit’).
top_stories.enhanced_summary: OpenAI rushed into a deal with the Pentagon to provide AI models for classified environments, following a failed negotiation between the Department of Defense and Anthropic, and a subsequent directive to cease using Anthropic's technology. Concerns arose about OpenAI's safeguards, leading the company to outline specific prohibited uses, including mass surveillance and autonomous weapons.
top_stories.why_matters: This deal highlights the increasing interest of the US government in leveraging AI for defense purposes and the competitive landscape between AI providers like OpenAI and Anthropic. It also raises ethical questions about the application of AI in sensitive areas like national security, forcing companies to define clear boundaries for their technology to avoid misuse and potential public backlash. The government's apparent preference also signals a potential shift in vendor landscape.

top_stories.title: SaaS in, SaaS out: Here’s what’s driving the SaaSpocalypse
top_stories.description: What's behind the SaaSpocalypse? It simply seems a new supreme has risen.
top_stories.scraped_content: Founder Summit 2026 in Boston:Don’t miss ticket savings of up to $300. Save up to $680 on your Disrupt 2026 pass. Latest AI Amazon Apps Biotech & Health Climate Cloud Computing Commerce Crypto Enterprise EVs Fintech Fundraising Gadgets Gaming Google Government & Policy Hardware Instagram Layoffs Media & Entertainment Meta Microsoft Privacy Robotics Security Social Space Startups TikTok Transportation Venture Staff Events Startup Battlefield StrictlyVC Newsletters Podcasts Videos Partner Content TechCrunch Brand Studio Crunchboard Contact Us One day not long ago, a founder texted his investor with an update: he was replacing his entire customer service team with Claude Code, an AI tool that can write and deploy software on its own. To Lex Zhao, an investor at One Way Ventures, the message indicated something bigger — the moment when companies like Salesforce stopped being the automatic default. “The barriers to entry for creating software are so low now thanks to coding agents, that the build versus buy decision is shifting toward build in so many cases,” Zhao told TechCrunch. The build versus buy shift is only part of the problem. The whole idea of using AI agents instead of people to perform work throws into question the SaaS business model itself. SaaS companies currently price their software per seat — meaning by how many employees log in to use it.
top_stories.enhanced_summary: A founder's decision to replace their customer service team with an AI coding tool signals a potential shift away from relying on established SaaS solutions like Salesforce. Investor Lex Zhao of One Way Ventures believes this shift towards building in-house solutions, driven by the accessibility of AI coding agents, threatens the traditional SaaS business model.
top_stories.why_matters: This signals a potentially seismic shift in how businesses approach software solutions, driven by advancements in AI. If companies increasingly opt to build instead of buy, the traditional per-seat SaaS model could face significant disruption, forcing providers to adapt or risk obsolescence. Business professionals should pay attention, as this could impact software budgets, IT strategies, and even employment structures.

quick_reads.title: Investors spill what they aren’t looking for anymore in AI SaaS companies
quick_reads.description: TechCrunch spoke with VCs to learn what investors aren't looking for in AI SaaS startups anymore.
quick_reads.llm_reason: Insights into what AI SaaS investors are avoiding can inform NZ businesses developing or seeking investment in this area.

quick_reads.title: Google looks to tackle longstanding RCS spam in India — but not alone
quick_reads.description: Google is integrating carrier-level filtering into RCS in India through a partnership with Airtel to strengthen protections against spam.
quick_reads.llm_reason: Worth monitoring trend in India to combat Spam in RCS channels

quick_reads.title: Anthropic’s Claude rises to No. 1 in the App Store following Pentagon dispute
quick_reads.description: Anthropic’s chatbot Claude seems to have benefited from the attention around the company’s fraught negotiations with the Pentagon.
quick_reads.llm_reason: The rise of Anthropic's Claude indicates the shifting competitive landscape in the AI chatbot market and is worth watching.
